Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic because I work and have other responsibilities that come first. I tried to carve out time on weekends and late evenings when my kids were asleep, but it wasn't consistent. I'm a visual learner, so I'd print out diagrams and charts when I could afford the ink, then study from those papers throughout the week. Some weeks I'd manage 18 hours, other weeks barely anything depending on what life threw at me.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because my work hours change and family needs come first. When assignments were due, I'd push hard and sacrifice sleep to get things done. It wasn't ideal, but at 29 with limited resources, you learn to be flexible and grab study time whenever it appears.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due or when I needed to download materials to study offline later. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in when I had a quiet moment and wanted to see if there was anything new I needed to prepare for. It wasn't about browsing—I went in with a purpose because my time is limited.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a smooth routine for me. There were stretches where I'd log in every few days to check announcements and download videos, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd disappear for a while. The closest I got to routine was Sunday nights—I'd try to plan my week and see what needed attention. But calling it a normal routine would be generous.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful because I could download them and watch during my commute or when I had downtime. I'm a visual learner, so seeing concepts explained with diagrams really helped things click. I also found some of the discussion posts helpful when other students shared their perspectives—it made me feel less alone in struggling with certain topics. The videos I could pause and rewatch were invaluable since I couldn't always attend live sessions.
Q6: I often skipped the optional readings and some of the supplementary materials because I simply didn't have time. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it went to the bottom of my priority list. I also didn't engage much with some interactive activities that required being online at specific times—my schedule just didn't allow for that. I had to be strategic about where I put my limited energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be tested on or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I also valued things that I could do on my own schedule, not activities that required me to be online at a specific time. If I could see the practical application or knew it would help my grade, I made time for it. Otherwise, I had to let it go.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they affected my grade, and passing this course matters too much to me. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice' but then reality would hit—I have bills to pay and kids to feed. The required work got done even when I was exhausted; the optional stuff only happened if stars aligned.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print what I could, and study from physical notes on the bus or during my lunch break at work. I also used YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't understand from the course materials. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab everything I needed when I had access and then work offline. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual study time.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours going over printed notes or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd work through problems in a notebook while my kids did their homework, completely disconnected from the internet. Sometimes I'd study with a coworker who was taking similar courses, quizzing each other during breaks. All that learning was invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it downloaded correctly, then watch it three or four more times offline over the following days. Same with readings—I'd access them once to save them, then study from the saved files repeatedly. My actual engagement with materials was much deeper than what any single access would show. One download often meant hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely periods when everything felt impossible. When my hours got cut at work, the financial stress made it hard to focus on anything academic. I also had a family health situation that took me away from studying for almost two weeks. During those times, I felt sad and overwhelmed, but I kept telling myself I'd catch up. My resilience got tested, but I refused to give up completely.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue was probably the biggest one—I'd log into the LMS late at night after a long day and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and something urgent at home. When I was confused about course material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because facing what I didn't understand felt overwhelming on top of everything else.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos or readings when I was too tired to really focus, just trying to feel like I was doing something productive. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Sometimes I accessed materials as a way to calm my anxiety about falling behind, even though I knew I wasn't in the right headspace to learn. It was more about going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was the sadness that would creep in and make everything feel pointless for a day or two. I'd tell myself I'd feel more capable tomorrow, and sometimes that was true, but sometimes tomorrow brought its own problems. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I might have drifted indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly find energy I didn't know I had and power through. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived. The pressure of a deadline cut through the fog of exhaustion and competing priorities.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. It reminded me that my efforts were being seen and that I was capable of doing this. Negative feedback was harder—sometimes it made me want to avoid that area of the course for a while. But overall, knowing someone was paying attention to my work made me feel more connected to the course and more willing to engage.
Q18: The structure was challenging for me because a lot of it assumed students could engage regularly and consistently. My attendance was low not because I didn't care, but because I couldn't always be there. Having eight online courses meant I was constantly juggling. The asynchronous elements were a lifesaver—without those, I couldn't have managed at all. But some structured activities just didn't fit my reality.
Q19: The ability to download materials made engagement possible for me—that was huge. But timed activities or things that required real-time participation were nearly impossible with my schedule. Discussion boards were okay because I could contribute when I had a moment. What made it harder was when important information was only shared during live sessions I couldn't attend. I'd find out about things too late sometimes.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading everything, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, something blocks them—fear of failure, perfectionism, or just running out of time. High activity might show genuine effort even when the final submissions don't come together. Life gets complicated.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I passed with decent exam scores because so much of my studying happened offline. I'd download what I needed and then disappear from the system while actually learning intensively. The LMS couldn't see me on the bus with my printed notes or studying at the kitchen table after midnight. Low activity doesn't mean low effort.
Q22: A course log can show when someone accessed materials and maybe patterns in their timing. It can flag if someone disappears completely. But it misses everything that happens offline, the quality of engagement when someone is online, and the context of someone's life. It can't tell if I watched a video while fully focused or while half-asleep. It definitely can't capture the emotional journey of trying to learn while life keeps happening.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about your own learning, even when it's hard. It's not just showing up—it's wrestling with concepts, asking yourself questions, and pushing through when you want to quit. For me, engagement looked like sacrificing sleep to review notes, thinking about course concepts while doing dishes, and refusing to give up even when I felt sad and overwhelmed. It's internal, not just visible.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how much this course meant to me or how hard I fought to stay in it. It couldn't capture the tears of frustration or the small victories when something finally clicked. It didn't see me choosing homework over rest, or the pride I felt when I passed an exam. My resilience, my determination to build a better life at 29—none of that shows up in login data. The numbers tell such a small part of my story.
